required.title: [For Mentor] The Whole-Brain Child
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of patient, careful people

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] The Story of Ferdinand
suggested.author: Munro Leaf

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Last Child in the Woods
supplementary.author: Richard Louv

competence_markers.description: They can sustain focused work on a single task for 15–20+ minutes without redirection, on most days
They complete tasks before switching to new ones — the habit of closure before transition is established
They participate in daily quiet time without distress or repeated complaints, and can occupy themselves productively during it
They listen fully when someone is speaking — body oriented, hands still, able to narrate back what was said
They function in device-free zones and times without negotiation or agitation
They can identify when their attention has wandered and bring it back — the beginnings of metacognitive awareness about focus
They show increasing capacity over weeks and months — the trendline of focus duration is moving upward
They can articulate, in their own words, what focus feels like and why it matters

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it feel like when you are really focused on something?
narration_prompts.detail: Not bored, not distracted — fully into it. What were you doing? What was it like?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the hardest thing about paying attention to one thing?
narration_prompts.detail: What makes you want to stop or switch? What do you do when that happens?
What do you do during quiet time? Is it hard to be without screens and noise? Has it gotten easier?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does full listening look like?
narration_prompts.detail: How is it different from half-listening? Can you tell when someone is not really listening to you? How?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you finished something all the way, even when you wanted to stop or switch.
narration_prompts.detail: What was it? Why did you keep going? How did it feel when you finished?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do we have device-free zones?
narration_prompts.detail: Not just "because the rules say so" — why do you think it matters? What is different about those times and spaces?

cross_connections.description: The focused work block lives inside the daily structure IC2.1 builds. The routine provides the when; IC2.3 provides the how — how to use that time with full presence rather than distracted half-effort.

Quality requires attention. The child who rushes through work because they cannot sustain focus produces work that does not meet the standard. IC2.3 builds the attentional capacity that IC2.2's standards demand.
The "stop" skill from IC1.1-A1 is the foundation of IC2.3's attention control. Stopping one thing to hold attention on another is the same inhibitory circuitry. IC2.3 extends what IC1.1-A1 began.

Frustration and boredom are the primary emotional barriers to sustained attention at this age. IC3.1's emotional regulation skills directly support IC2.3's focused work blocks. The child who can tolerate boredom can sustain focus.

cross_connections.target_name: Digital Systems (especially 4.2.6 — Attention, Addiction & Digital Discipline)
cross_connections.description: IC2.3 builds the internal capacity for attention. 4.2.6 addresses the external system designed to capture and fragment it. Together, they form a complete defense — internal strength and external awareness.

Math requires sustained cognitive attention — the earliest formal training in holding a thought, working through a problem, and not giving up when the answer is not immediately obvious. Every math session is an IC2.3 training session.

Writing requires the same single-tasking and completion discipline that IC2.3 develops. The child who can sustain focus on a writing task produces more and better work than the child whose attention fragments midway through a sentence.

content.why_this_matters:
You live in a world that does not want you to pay attention. Not really. It wants pieces of your attention — a few seconds here, a glance there, a quick reaction before you move on to the next thing. Everything around you is designed to interrupt. Screens flash. Sounds play. New things appear before you have finished the old thing. And none of this is accidental. People built it this way on purpose, because your attention is worth money to them.

This packet is about something different. It is about learning to give your whole mind to one thing at a time. That sounds simple. It is not. It is one of the hardest things you will learn — harder than reading, harder than math, harder than most physical skills. Because the world will fight you on it every single day.

Here is why it matters: **a person who cannot pay attention is a person who can be controlled.** If someone else decides what you look at, what you think about, and when you stop thinking about it, then someone else is running your mind. You are not free. You are a passenger. The person who can choose what to pay attention to — and hold that attention as long as they decide — is the person who can think their own thoughts, finish their own work, and become someone real instead of someone managed.

That is what you are building here. Not just the ability to sit still. The ability to own your own mind.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Single-tasking** — the ability to do one thing at a time, fully, before moving to the next
2. **Short focused work blocks** — sustained attention on one task for 10–15 minutes, gradually extending
3. **Quiet time tolerance** — the ability to be in a low-stimulation environment without distress or restlessness
4. **Full listening** — when someone is speaking, giving them your whole attention, not partial attention
5. **Completion before transition** — finishing what you are doing before starting something new
6. **Device-free presence** — being fully present in spaces and times where screens are absent
content.core_content:
### Part 1: One Thing at a Time — The Foundation of All Focus

Focus begins with a single idea: **one thing at a time.**

This is not a suggestion. It is the way your brain actually works. Your brain cannot pay attention to two things at the same time. When people think they are doing two things at once — watching something while doing homework, talking while building, listening to instructions while playing — they are not doing both things. They are switching between them very quickly, and doing both of them badly.

Adults call this "multitasking" and many of them believe they are good at it. They are wrong. Research has shown this over and over: people who multitask produce worse work, make more mistakes, and take longer to finish than people who do one thing at a time. Every time your brain switches from one thing to another, it costs time and energy — like stopping a car, turning around, driving in a new direction, then stopping again and turning back. The more you switch, the more energy you waste.

**What one thing at a time looks like:**
- When you are building with blocks, you are building with blocks. Not also talking to someone across the room. Not also looking at what someone else is doing. Building.
- When you are listening to someone read aloud, you are listening. Your hands are still. Your eyes are on the reader or closed. Your mind is on the story.
- When you are eating, you are eating. Not also playing. Not also watching something. Eating.
- When you are doing your schoolwork, you are doing your schoolwork. The other things you want to do will still be there when you finish.

This feels strange at first if you are used to always having something else going on. That strangeness is the feeling of your brain learning a new way to work. It will pass. What replaces it is something better: the experience of being fully present in what you are doing, which makes the work better, faster, and more satisfying.

### Part 2: The Focused Work Block — Training Your Attention Like a Muscle

Your ability to focus is like a muscle. Right now, it may be small. That is fine. All muscles start small. What matters is that you train it — regularly, consistently, a little more each time.

**The focused work block is how you train it.**

A focused work block is a set period of time where you work on one thing, with no interruptions, no distractions, and no switching. When the block is over, you stop. But during the block, you are fully in.

**For younger students (ages 4–6):** Start with 10 minutes. Ten minutes of focused, uninterrupted work on one task. No getting up. No looking around for something else. No asking "how much longer?" Just the work, for ten minutes. When you can do 10 minutes consistently — most days, without needing to be redirected — extend to 12. Then 15. Then 20.

**For older Stretching Branches students (ages 7–9):** Start wherever you are. If 10 minutes is easy, start at 15. The target by the end of Stretching Branches is 20–25 minutes of sustained, productive focus. This is not sitting still for 25 minutes — it is working productively for 25 minutes. There is a difference. Sitting still while your mind wanders is not focus. Engaging fully with the work in front of you is.

**What happens during a focused work block:**
- A timer is set. The student knows how long the block is.
- The work is in front of them. Nothing else is.
- No screens unless the work requires them (and at Stretching Branches, it almost never should).
- No conversations unless they are about the work.
- If the student finishes early, they continue with something related or sit quietly until the timer ends. They do not switch to entertainment.
- When the timer goes off, the block is over. The student takes a real break — movement, a drink of water, a few minutes of free time.

**The progression matters more than the duration.** A student who reliably does 12 minutes of real focus every day is building more capacity than a student who occasionally does 30 minutes with frequent interruptions. Consistency is the training stimulus. Duration is the result.

### Part 3: Quiet Time — The Space Where Attention Grows

Quiet time is not punishment. It is not timeout. It is a regular, daily period of low stimulation where the child is alone with their own thoughts and their own chosen activity.

**Why it matters:** Your brain needs periods of low input to develop the internal wiring that produces sustained attention. A brain that is constantly receiving stimulation — sounds, images, voices, entertainment — never develops the ability to generate its own focus. It becomes dependent on external input the way a person who is always carried never develops the strength to walk.

**What quiet time looks like:**
- A period of 20–45 minutes (depending on age) each day
- No screens. None.
- Low noise — no music playing, no television in the background, no one talking to the child
- The child chooses their activity: reading, drawing, building, playing with toys, looking out the window, sitting and thinking
- The adult is not directing the activity or interacting during this time
- The child is not being entertained. They are entertaining themselves — or, more importantly, learning that they do not always need to be entertained

**For younger students:** Quiet time may feel uncomfortable at first. "I'm bored" will appear quickly. Boredom is not a problem. It is the doorway. The child who pushes through "I'm bored" and finds something to engage with on their own has just exercised their attention muscle in one of the most important ways possible. Do not rescue them from boredom. Boredom is the teacher.

**For older Stretching Branches students:** Quiet time should become a valued part of the day — a period the child actually looks forward to because they have learned what they can do with uninterrupted time and their own mind. If this has not happened yet, the environment may need adjustment (too much stimulation elsewhere in the day making quiet time feel like deprivation by contrast).

### Part 4: Listening Fully — Attention as Respect

When someone is speaking to you, that is your task. Not half your task. Your entire task.

Full listening means:
- Your body is turned toward the speaker
- Your eyes are on them (or, for younger children, generally oriented toward them)
- Your hands are still — not fidgeting with something, not playing with something, not doing something else
- You are not waiting for them to finish so you can say your thing. You are hearing what they are saying.
- When they are done, you can tell them what they said — not word for word, but the idea

This is harder than it sounds. Most adults cannot do it consistently. Watch people in a conversation — most of them are thinking about what they want to say next while the other person is still talking. They are not listening. They are waiting.

**Why this matters beyond politeness:** Listening is the first form of attention you practice with another person. A child who learns to listen fully — to actually receive what someone is communicating — develops the neural pathways of sustained attention in a social context. These are the same pathways they will use to read carefully, follow complex instructions, understand a lecture, and eventually think through a difficult argument.

A child who cannot listen fully will struggle with reading comprehension, will miss instructions and need them repeated, will have difficulty in group work, and will eventually have difficulty in relationships. Listening is not a social nicety. It is an attention skill that scales into everything.

**Practice:** During read-alouds, family conversations, and instruction times, the standard is full listening. This is enforced gently but consistently. If the child is fidgeting, redirect once: "Hands still. Listen." If they cannot recall what was said, it indicates attention was elsewhere. The response is not anger — it is a clear observation: "You were not listening. That is something we are working on."

### Part 5: Completing Before Switching — The Habit of Closure

You have already practiced this in IC1.1-A1 — completing tasks fully before moving on. IC2.3 deepens it by connecting task completion to attention discipline.

**The principle:** Every time you stop something before it is done and start something new, you are training your brain that incomplete is normal. Your brain begins to expect switching. It begins to resist sustained effort because it has learned that something new and more interesting is always about to arrive. Over time, this becomes a person who cannot finish anything — not because they lack ability, but because their brain has been trained to abandon.

**What completing before switching looks like at Stretching Branches:**
- If you are drawing a picture, you finish the picture before starting a new activity. "Finish" does not mean "perfect" — it means you have completed what you set out to do.
- If you are working through a set of math problems, you do all of them before moving on. Not most of them. All of them.
- If you are reading a chapter, you read the chapter. Not half the chapter.
- If you are building something, you build it to completion — or, if it is a multi-session project, you reach a defined stopping point before you switch.

**The exception:** If the focused work block timer ends and the task is not complete, the student stops and notes where they left off so they can return. This is not abandonment — it is a planned pause within a structure. The difference between stopping because the structure says stop and stopping because you got bored is the entire difference between discipline and impulse.

### Part 6: Device-Free Zones — Protecting Attention by Design

Certain spaces and times in your home are device-free. This is not because screens are evil. It is because screens are engineered to capture attention — and in spaces and times dedicated to focused work, family conversation, meals, or quiet time, that capture works against everything you are building.

**Device-free zones (non-negotiable):**
- The table where you eat meals
- The space where you do focused work
- Bedrooms during sleep time
- Quiet time

**Device-free times (non-negotiable):**
- During focused work blocks
- During meals
- During quiet time
- During read-alouds and family conversation
- The first hour after waking and the last hour before sleep

**Why this matters for attention development:** Every time a screen is present, part of your brain is attending to it — even if you are not looking at it. Research shows that the mere presence of a phone on a table reduces cognitive performance, even when the phone is face down and silent. The device does not need to be active to fragment your attention. It only needs to be there, because your brain knows it could become active.

Removing devices from spaces is not deprivation. It is environmental design. A person who is trying to focus in a room full of devices is like a person trying to sleep in a room full of light. The conditions are wrong. Fix the conditions, and the capacity emerges.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Daily Focused Work Block [Daily]
Set a timer. Work on one task. No interruptions. Start at 10–15 minutes and extend as capacity grows. Track the duration weekly — not with pressure, but with awareness. The question is: can you stay in the work for the full time? Over weeks, the answer becomes yes for longer and longer periods.

### Practice 2: Quiet Time [Daily]
20–45 minutes of low-stimulation, self-directed time. No screens. No one directing the activity. The child chooses what to do. If they say "I'm bored," the answer is: "That is okay. See what happens." Boredom is not the problem. It is the doorway to self-generated focus.

### Practice 3: Full Listening Practice [Daily, During Conversations and Read-Alouds]
During read-alouds, family meals, and instruction times, practice full listening. Body oriented. Hands still. After the speaker finishes, the child narrates back what was said. Not as a test — as a practice. The narration reveals whether attention was present.

### Practice 4: One-Task Completion [Daily, During All Work]
The rule is applied to all work: finish before you switch. When the child moves to start something new before finishing, redirect: "Finish that first." Over time, the child begins to self-enforce. That internalization is the goal.

### Practice 5: Device-Free Zones Enforcement [Daily, Environmental]
Maintain device-free zones and times consistently. The child does not negotiate these. The adult does not make exceptions for convenience. The zones and times are structural — part of the architecture of the home, not a rule that varies by mood.

### Practice 6: Attention Check-In [Weekly, With Mentor]
Once a week, during the weekly review: "How did your focus go this week? When was it hardest to pay attention? What pulled you away? When was it easiest? What were you doing?" This builds the habit of noticing attention — of being aware of where your mind is, which is the precursor to governing where it goes.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Your Environment Is Their Curriculum

A child cannot develop sustained attention in a home where screens are always on, background noise is constant, and stimulation is the default state. This is not a character judgment — it is neuroscience. The developing brain wires itself to the environment it lives in. A brain raised in constant stimulation develops for constant stimulation: rapid switching, shallow processing, immediate reward. A brain raised with periods of quiet, sustained engagement, and low stimulation develops the circuitry for depth.

**Audit your home environment honestly:**
- Is there a television on in the background during the day, even if no one is "watching" it?
- Is music playing constantly?
- Does the child have access to a phone, tablet, or screen during unstructured time?
- How many toys are available in the child's play space at any one time? (More than 10–15 items creates choice overload that prevents sustained engagement.)
- When the child says "I'm bored," how quickly does someone provide entertainment?

Each "yes" is not a failure — it is a variable you can change. The environmental design comes before the attention training. Fix the environment first. Then the training has ground to grow in.

### The Adult's Attention Models Everything

The child who sees you scrolling your phone during dinner will not take device-free zones seriously. The child who sees you check a notification mid-conversation will learn that attention is interruptible. The child who sees you unable to sit without a screen will not learn to sit without a screen.

This is uncomfortable. You may be as captured by your devices as the child. The difference is that you have the capacity to choose differently — and the child is watching whether you do.

**The non-negotiable:** During device-free times and zones, your devices are gone too. Not face-down. Gone. Out of the room. If you cannot do this, you have diagnosed your own attention problem, and addressing it is the most important thing you can do for your child's development in IC2.3.

### Stimulation Reduction Is Not Deprivation

Reducing screens, noise, and stimulation is not depriving the child. It is removing the artificial competition that prevents natural engagement from developing. A child with three toys available plays more deeply than a child with thirty. A child in a quiet room engages more fully than a child in a noisy one. A child who is bored for ten minutes and then finds something to do has just accomplished something a child who was immediately handed a screen never had the opportunity to accomplish.

Expect pushback. The child accustomed to stimulation will protest its removal. This is not evidence that the child needs stimulation — it is evidence of how deeply the dependency has formed. The protest is temporary. What replaces it, over days and weeks, is the capacity for self-generated engagement that is the foundation of all focus.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Focused Work Block Duration

**Developing well:** The child sustains focused work on a single task for age-appropriate duration (10–15 minutes for ages 4–6, 15–25 minutes for ages 7–9) without redirection on most days. The duration is gradually increasing over weeks. The child enters the work block without resistance and works productively — not just sitting still, but engaging with the material.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot sustain 10 minutes of focused work without getting up, switching tasks, or requiring redirection. Duration is not improving over weeks. The child resists entering work blocks or treats them as endurance tests rather than productive work.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the work appropriate for the child's level? (Too easy produces boredom; too hard produces frustration. Both look like attention problems but are task-design problems.) Is the environment set up for focus? (A workspace with toys visible, siblings moving through, or noise from another room is an environment designed for distraction.) Is the child getting enough physical activity? (A child who has been sitting all morning cannot focus in the afternoon. Move the body first.)

### 2. Quiet Time

**Developing well:** The child enters quiet time without significant resistance. They find an activity within a few minutes and engage with it. "I'm bored" may still appear occasionally but does not persist — the child moves through it. The child may actually begin to look forward to quiet time.

**Developing slowly:** The child protests every quiet time. They cannot find anything to do. Boredom escalates to distress. They repeatedly leave the quiet time space or seek adult interaction.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the rest of the day overstimulating? (A child on screens for two hours and then placed in quiet time experiences withdrawal, not boredom. Reduce screens overall, not just during quiet time.) Is the quiet time space conducive? (A room with engaging, accessible, non-screen activities — books, art supplies, building materials, puzzles — makes self-directed engagement possible. An empty room does not.) Has the adult been consistent? (If quiet time is enforced some days and not others, the child learns that resistance works.)

### 3. Single-Tasking

**Developing well:** The child works on one thing at a time. When they are building, they are building. When they are drawing, they are drawing. They complete their current activity before starting a new one, most of the time.

**Developing slowly:** The child bounces between activities rapidly — starts one thing, abandons it, starts another, abandons that. Multiple activities are open simultaneously. Nothing is completed.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child accustomed to screens that provide constant switching? (Screen use trains rapid attention switching. If the child watches content with fast cuts and scene changes, their brain is being trained for exactly the behavior you are trying to change. Reduce screen-based stimulation.) Are there too many options available? (Reduce the number of toys and activities accessible at one time. Fewer options produce deeper engagement.)

### 4. Full Listening

**Developing well:** During read-alouds and conversations, the child is oriented toward the speaker, hands are relatively still, and they can narrate back the content with reasonable accuracy. They wait for their turn to speak.

**Developing slowly:** The child fidgets constantly during listening, looks elsewhere, interrupts, and cannot recall what was said. They "listen" while doing something else.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the listening periods age-appropriate in length? (A 4-year-old can listen fully for 5–10 minutes. A 9-year-old for 15–20. Beyond that, you are testing endurance, not attention.) Is the content engaging? (A child who cannot listen to a dry explanation but listens perfectly to a compelling story does not have an attention problem — they have a content problem. Make the content worth attending to.) Does the adult model full listening? (When the child speaks to you, do you stop what you are doing and listen fully? Or do you half-listen while doing something else?)

### 5. Completion Before Switching

**Developing well:** The child finishes tasks before beginning new ones. When tempted to switch, they can be redirected once and return to the current task. The habit of closure is developing — the child shows a sense of satisfaction at finishing.

**Developing slowly:** Tasks are routinely abandoned. Redirection to the current task produces resistance. The child leaves a trail of half-finished projects and activities.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the task appropriately scoped? (A task that takes 45 minutes to complete is not a single task for a 5-year-old — it is five tasks. Break work into completable units so the child can experience the closure of finishing.) Is the environment providing competing stimulation? (If a sibling starts something interesting while the child is working, the pull to switch is environmental, not characterological. Manage the environment.)

### 6. Device-Free Zone Compliance

**Developing well:** The child functions in device-free zones without requesting screens, without negotiating, and without agitation. These zones are normal — part of how the home operates.

**Developing slowly:** Every device-free period is a battle. The child asks for screens repeatedly. The absence of devices produces visible distress or escalating behavior.

**Critical note:** A child who is significantly distressed by the absence of screens — not mildly annoyed, but genuinely distressed — may have developed a dependency that requires more than IC2.3. Consider a structured screen elimination period (not reduction — elimination) under mentor guidance. The distress level during removal is diagnostic: it tells you how deep the dependency goes.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Using Screens as a Focus Tool
"But they focus really well when they're on the iPad." This is not focus. This is stimulation-capture — the device is doing the attending for the brain, providing such intense and rapidly changing stimulation that the brain does not need to generate its own attention. The child "focused" on a screen is in a state neurologically opposite to the focus IC2.3 builds. If a child can attend to a screen for an hour but cannot attend to a book for ten minutes, the screen is the problem, not the solution.

### Mistaking Compliance for Focus
A child who sits still and quiet during a work block may be focused — or may be daydreaming, zoning out, or just enduring the clock. Focus is not the absence of movement. It is the presence of engaged attention. Check the output: is the child producing work during the work block? Is the work of reasonable quality? If the child sits quietly for fifteen minutes and produces nothing, they were not focused. They were waiting.

### Rescuing from Boredom
When the child says "I'm bored" during quiet time, the temptation is to suggest an activity, provide a toy, or shorten the period. Every rescue teaches the child that boredom is someone else's problem to solve. Boredom is the gap between external stimulation ending and internal engagement beginning. The child must cross that gap themselves. Your job is to ensure the environment has appropriate materials available, and then to leave them in the gap.

### Inconsistent Device-Free Enforcement
The device-free rule that applies on Monday but not on a busy Wednesday trains the child that rules are conditional. If devices appear during a device-free time because the adult needs quiet, the lesson is: devices are the tool adults use when they cannot manage the situation. Enforce consistently or do not set the rule. A rule that varies is worse than no rule because it teaches that stated standards are negotiable.

### Over-Scheduling
A child whose day is packed with activities, lessons, transitions, and structured engagement has no room for sustained focus to develop. Focus requires unstructured time — time where the child must generate their own attention rather than having it directed by an instructor or program. If every waking hour is filled with something, there is no space for the quiet, self-directed engagement that builds depth. Leave gaps. The gaps are where the work happens.

### Comparing to Screens
"You can focus on your Legos for an hour. Why can't you focus on math for fifteen minutes?" Because Legos provide intrinsic reward — the creation is immediately visible, the feedback is constant, the engagement is self-paced. Math provides none of these things naturally. The capacity to focus on non-rewarding, cognitively demanding work is built slowly and is a different skill than the capacity to focus on something inherently engaging. Both are valuable. The first is much harder to develop.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What Focus Feels Like
**Strong:** The child describes a specific experience — "When I was building my castle, I forgot about everything else and just kept building. It felt like time went fast." Shows awareness of the internal experience of focus.
**Weak:** "I don't know" or "Focus means paying attention." No personal reference, no internal experience described.

### Prompt 2: Hardest Thing About Paying Attention
**Strong:** Names a specific challenge — "I keep wanting to look at what my sister is doing" or "My brain wants to think about playing outside." Shows metacognitive awareness of distraction.
**Weak:** "Nothing" or "It's easy." Either not yet self-aware enough to identify the struggle, or not engaged honestly with the question.

### Prompt 3: Quiet Time
**Strong:** Describes what they do during quiet time with some detail. Acknowledges it was hard at first but may be easier now. Shows that they can occupy themselves.
**Weak:** "I just sit there" or "I hate it." If the child genuinely hates quiet time after weeks of practice, the environment or implementation may need adjustment.

### Prompt 4: Full Listening
**Strong:** Can distinguish between full listening and half-listening from their own experience. "Full listening is when I hear everything. Half-listening is when I'm also thinking about something else and then I miss parts." Can tell when others are not really listening to them.
**Weak:** "Listening is when you hear someone." No distinction between levels of attention.

### Prompt 5: Finishing Something Hard
**Strong:** Names a specific instance. Describes the temptation to stop and the choice to continue. Describes the feeling of completion. "I wanted to stop my math problems because they were hard, but I kept going and I got them all done. I felt proud."
**Weak:** Cannot recall an instance, or all examples are of things that were easy and fun.

### Prompt 6: Why Device-Free Zones Exist
**Strong:** Goes beyond "because the rules say so." Shows some understanding of why: "Because screens make it harder to pay attention" or "So we can be together without looking at things." The child has internalized some of the reasoning, not just the rule.
**Weak:** "Because you said so." Compliance without understanding. This is acceptable at ages 4–5 but should be developing toward understanding by 7–9.
